title: Analytical Model for Heat Transfer Accounting for Both Conduction and Dispersion in Aquifers with a Robin-Type Boundary Condition at the Injection Well

research.question: How can radial heat transport during heated-water injection be represented when aquifer conduction and dispersion act together and heat flux must remain continuous at the injection-well rim?
research.system: A fully penetrating injection well in a finite-thickness confined aquifer bounded vertically by bedrock and caprock, with radial aquifer convection, conduction, and dispersion coupled to vertical conduction in the confining rocks.
research.inputData: Injection rate and temperature history, aquifer and confining-rock thermal properties, well and layer geometry, finite-difference benchmarks, five existing analytical solutions, and measured temperatures from four observation wells at the Mobile, Alabama ATES experiment.
research.method: Form a three-domain heat-transfer model, impose a Robin well boundary from heat-flux continuity, obtain semianalytical Laplace-domain solutions and dominant-process limits, and examine numerical, sensitivity, regime, and field comparisons.
research.benchmark: The present solution matches the finite-difference temperature field and is compared with the Chen-Reddell, Voigt-Haefner, Guimera, Li, and Zhou solutions before evaluation against the Mobile ATES observations.
research.uncertainty: The model assumes homogeneous isotropic media, thermal equilibrium between solid and fluid, no phase change, negligible buoyancy and regional groundwater flow, and negligible vertical aquifer conduction; the field comparison is affected by injection-rate averaging and aquifer heterogeneity.
research.conclusion: Conduction and dispersion cannot generally be substituted for one another: their joint treatment and the Robin well condition matter near the well, injection and aquifer properties dominate sensitivity, and the model gives reasonably good agreement with the Mobile injection-period temperatures within the stated assumptions.

narrative.throughline: Follow injected heat from the well rim through a radially spreading aquifer and vertically exchanging confining rocks, then test when conduction, dispersion, or both control the observed thermal plume.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: A radial injection plume is commonly pictured as advection with either conductive or dispersive spreading and a prescribed well temperature.
narrative.stakes: Choosing one spreading mechanism or forcing the well temperature can distort near-well temperatures and the inferred reach of an ATES thermal plume.
narrative.resolution: The coupled Robin-boundary solution reconnects process regimes, numerical agreement, parameter sensitivity, and Mobile field temperatures to the same aquifer-well heat-flow object.

background.premise: Earlier radial injection solutions did not represent thermal conduction and thermal dispersion simultaneously.
background.visibleEvidence: Two separate legacy plume envelopes merge into one radial temperature field with conductive and dispersive widths.
background.whyNow: The missing joint process representation motivates the proposed ATES heat-transfer model.
background.boundary: This premise concerns the radially divergent aquifer models reviewed by the paper, not every numerical heat-transport model.
background.sourceExcerpt: Obviously, the past analytical models for the heat transfer induced by well injection in an aquifer did not consider the processes of thermal conduction and thermal dispersion at the same time.
background.visualObject: Radial aquifer plume split into conduction-only and dispersion-only outlines
background.motionPurpose: Expose the missing overlap between the two heat-spreading processes
background.stepDetail: Hold the injection front fixed while the two incomplete outlines expand separately.
background.transitionBridge: Merge both outlines at the well rim to introduce the new model.

background.premise: A constant-temperature injection boundary does not conserve heat-flux continuity at the wellbore.
background.visibleEvidence: Incoming pipe heat flux meets the aquifer-side conductive, dispersive, and convective flux arrows at the well rim.
background.whyNow: A Robin boundary is needed to couple the injected heat supply to aquifer transport.
background.boundary: The Dirichlet limit remains applicable when convection dominates; the objection is to imposing it generally.
background.sourceExcerpt: In addition, the existing models commonly regarded the inner boundary at the injection well
background.visualObject: Injection-well rim with balanced inward and outward heat-flux arrows
background.motionPurpose: Replace a fixed temperature badge with a flux balance
background.stepDetail: Fade the prescribed-temperature clamp and reveal matching flux arrows across the rim.
background.transitionBridge: Carry the balanced rim into the aquifer-rock geometry.

background.premise: The aquifer and its bounding rocks have distinct thermal properties and exchange heat across their interfaces.
background.visibleEvidence: A horizontal aquifer layer is bracketed by bedrock and caprock with vertical heat arrows at both interfaces.
background.whyNow: The bounding rocks provide conductive heat storage beyond the radial aquifer domain.
background.boundary: The lower bedrock and upper caprock boundaries are held at constant temperature in the model.
background.sourceExcerpt: The aquifer and rocks have different thermal properties. The lower boundary of the bedrock and the upper boundary of the caprock are both assumed under a constant temperature condition.
background.motionPurpose: Establish vertical thermal coupling around the radial plume
background.stepDetail: Shade each layer by thermal role and pulse heat arrows through both interfaces.
background.transitionBridge: Lock the three layers into the mathematical domain.

methods.input: Finite aquifer thickness b, bedrock thickness b1, caprock thickness b2, well radius rw, and constant injection rate Q
methods.output: Fully penetrating injection well in an aquifer bounded by bedrock and caprock domains
methods.validity: The geometry assumes a homogeneous isotropic aquifer, local thermal equilibrium, constant outer-rock temperatures, and negligible phase change, buoyancy, regional flow, and vertical aquifer conduction.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1 Conceptual Model, Figure 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: The model is comprised of an aquifer of thickness b conﬁned by a bedrock of thickness b1 and a caprock of thickness b2. The well fully penetrates the aquifer with a radius rw and has a constant injection rate Q.
methods.motionPurpose: Convert the reported dimensions into the source conceptual geometry
methods.stepDetail: Label b, b1, b2, rw, and Q as the domains and well appear.
methods.transitionBridge: Zoom to the well rim where the domains receive injected heat.

methods.input: Injection-pipe heat flux J1 and aquifer-side heat flux J2 at r = rw
methods.output: Robin-type inner boundary condition coupling injection temperature to conduction, dispersion, and convection
methods.validity: The derivation enforces J1 = J2 at the injection-well rim.
methods.locator: PDF page 17, Appendix A, Equations A1-A3
methods.sourceExcerpt: According to the continuity of heat ﬂux at the injection well, that is, J1 = J2, one has
methods.motionPurpose: Turn physical flux continuity into the inner mathematical boundary
methods.stepDetail: Align J1 and J2 arrows and replace them with the Robin condition marker.
methods.transitionBridge: Expand the boundary marker across the coupled equations.

methods.input: Coupled time-dependent aquifer and rock heat equations
methods.output: Laplace-domain aquifer, bedrock, and caprock equations
methods.validity: The initial temperature rise is zero and the transformed boundary and interface conditions are retained.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The Laplace domain solutions to the model for heat transfer in the aquifer, bedrock, and caprock are derived using the Laplace transform method.
methods.visualObject: Three time-domain temperature fields mapped onto a Laplace plane
methods.motionPurpose: Make the coupled transient problem algebraically solvable
methods.stepDetail: Sweep a p-plane across all three domains while preserving interface links.
methods.transitionBridge: Collapse the transformed domains into their semianalytical temperature fields.

methods.input: Laplace-domain temperature solutions for the three media
methods.output: Time-domain aquifer, bedrock, and caprock temperatures obtained by numerical inverse Laplace transformation
methods.validity: The paper uses the Crump algorithm based on a Fourier-series approximation for inversion.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The time domain results of equations (19)–(21) can be inverted by a numerical inverse scheme called the Crump (1976) algorithm developed based on the Fourier series approximation.
methods.visualObject: Three Laplace-domain temperature solutions unfolding onto the time axis
methods.motionPurpose: Restore observable temperature fields from the transform domain
methods.stepDetail: Transform Equations 19 through 21 numerically with the Crump scheme.
methods.transitionBridge: Duplicate the full solution into limiting-process branches.

methods.input: The aquifer heat equation with effective conductivity lambda_a
methods.output: Dispersion-dominant aquifer equation with lambda_a approximated by lambda_d
methods.validity: This reduction applies where thermal dispersion greatly exceeds thermal conduction so lambda_a is approximated by lambda_d.
methods.sourceExcerpt: If thermal dispersion is dominant in the heat ﬂow ﬁeld, that is, λd ≫ λc in the ATES system, λa can be approximated as λd.
methods.visualObject: Effective-conductivity term collapsing onto its dispersive component
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the single reduction that defines the dispersion-dominant limit
methods.stepDetail: Replace lambda_a with lambda_d in the aquifer heat equation.
methods.transitionBridge: The reduced dispersion equation is solved in its own derivation step.

methods.output: Airy-function aquifer temperature solution in Equation (23)
methods.validity: The solution inherits the dispersion-dominant approximation lambda_d much greater than lambda_c.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The dispersion‐dominant solution for the aquifer can then be obtained as
methods.visualObject: Dispersion-dominant equation resolving into an Airy-function temperature profile
methods.motionPurpose: Separate the analytical solution derivation from the conductivity substitution.
methods.stepDetail: Solve Equation 22 for the transformed aquifer temperature.
methods.transitionBridge: The complementary conductivity substitution defines the conduction limit.

methods.input: The aquifer heat equation with effective conductivity lambda_a
methods.output: Conduction-dominant aquifer equation with lambda_a approximated by lambda_c
methods.validity: This reduction applies where thermal dispersion is much smaller than thermal conduction so lambda_a is approximated by lambda_c.
methods.sourceExcerpt: if thermal conduction is dominant, that is, λd ≪ λc, one has λa ≈ λc. Thus, equation (2) may be written as
methods.visualObject: Effective-conductivity term collapsing onto its conductive component
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the single reduction that defines the conduction-dominant limit
methods.stepDetail: Replace lambda_a with lambda_c in the aquifer heat equation.
methods.transitionBridge: The reduced conduction equation is solved in its own derivation step.

methods.output: Modified-Bessel aquifer temperature solution in Equation (25)
methods.validity: The solution inherits the conduction-dominant approximation lambda_d much smaller than lambda_c.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The conduction‐dominant solution for the aquifer can then be derived as
methods.visualObject: Conduction-dominant equation resolving into a modified-Bessel temperature profile
methods.motionPurpose: Separate the analytical solution derivation from the conductivity substitution.
methods.stepDetail: Solve Equation 24 for the transformed aquifer temperature.
methods.transitionBridge: The full and limiting solutions can now be checked against the numerical benchmark.

methods.input: Present semianalytical temperatures and finite-difference temperatures
methods.output: Agreement between the present solution and the finite-difference benchmark
methods.validity: The comparison covers the parameter cases shown in the paper's verification figures.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Finally, the temperature distribution predicted by the ﬁnite difference solution shows a very good match with that by the present solution.
methods.visualObject: Overlaid semianalytical and finite-difference radial temperature curves
methods.motionPurpose: Check solution agreement before physical interpretation
methods.stepDetail: Draw both curves from the well outward and highlight their overlap.
methods.transitionBridge: Keep the full curve fixed while the limiting curves separate by regime.

methods.input: Full, conduction-dominant, and dispersion-dominant solutions across Pe and beta/L
methods.output: A process-regime map identifying coupled and dominant heat-transfer regions
methods.validity: The dominant limits apply only where their curves agree with the full solution; intermediate cases require both processes.
methods.sourceExcerpt: implying that the effects of both conduction and dispersion should be considered.
methods.motionPurpose: Locate where coupled transport replaces either single-process limit
methods.stepDetail: Move a marker across conduction, coupled, and dispersion regions while updating the radial curve.
methods.transitionBridge: Carry the regime marker into a parameter-sensitivity grid.

methods.input: Ranges for Q, fluid and medium heat capacities, conductivities, layer thicknesses, and thermal dispersivity
methods.output: Relative input-parameter influence on aquifer temperature
methods.validity: The Morris screening analysis reflects the specified parameter ranges and temperature output cases.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The Morris (1991) method is chosen to evaluate the global sensitivity of the output temperature to the changes in each of the input parameters.
methods.motionPurpose: Rank the model inputs controlling the thermal response
methods.stepDetail: Perturb each parameter track and place its marker by sensitivity magnitude and interaction spread.
methods.transitionBridge: Retain the influential injection and aquifer parameters for the field test.

methods.input: First-cycle 31-day Mobile injection data, grand-average Q of 147 gpm, grand-average Tin of 58.2 degrees C, beta of 1.23 m, reported thermal properties, and temperatures at wells 1, 4, 7, and 10
methods.output: Field-scale temperature comparisons during heated-water injection
methods.validity: The comparison represents the first cycle's 31-day injection period, uses grand-average injection conditions in the present solution, assumes a homogeneous equivalent aquifer, and does not resolve vertically varied permeability or discontinuous early injection rates.
methods.locator: PDF pages 13-15, Section 3.5 A Field Experiment in Alabama, Figures 7-8
methods.sourceExcerpt: We now demonstrate the temperature distribution predicted by the present solution
methods.visualObject: Mobile injection well and four observation-well temperature traces
methods.motionPurpose: Return the analytical plume to measured aquifer temperatures
methods.stepDetail: Apply the reported averaged inputs, project the radial solution to four observation radii, and overlay each measured trace.
methods.transitionBridge: Pull the traces back into the complete aquifer-well cross section.

symbols.transition: Color the aquifer by temperature rise

symbols.meaning: Peclet number measuring convection relative to conduction
symbols.transition: Move the regime marker along log(Pe)

symbols.visualObject: Dominance ratio on the regime map
symbols.meaning: Ratio of dispersive to conductive thermal conductivity

return.visualObject: Mobile ATES aquifer cross section with Robin well rim, coupled thermal plume, confining-rock exchange, and four observation traces
return.claim: Within the modeled injection period, the coupled conduction-dispersion solution gives reasonably good agreement with the measured Mobile ATES temperature distribution.
return.claimBoundary: This agreement supports preliminary injection-period thermal analysis under the model assumptions; it does not resolve heterogeneous aquifer structure, abrupt injection-rate histories, buoyancy, regional flow, or recovery-cycle behavior.
return.locator: PDF page 15, Section 4 Concluding Remarks, finding 6
return.sourceExcerpt: In comparison to the measured temperature data, the present solution gives a reasonably good agree- ment in the temperature distribution for the ATES ﬁeld experiment site near Mobile, Alabama.
return.minimalText: Coupled heat flow matches the field

visual.primaryObject: Radial heated-water plume in a confined aquifer around a fully penetrating injection well
visual.accentObject: Robin well-rim flux balance and vertical heat exchange with bedrock and caprock
visual.measuredQuantity: Aquifer temperature versus time and radial distance
visual.geometryNotes: Use an axisymmetric aquifer slab of thickness b between bedrock b1 and caprock b2; transform from a close well-rim flux balance to radial temperature curves, a Pe-beta/L regime plane, and four Mobile observation radii before returning to the layered cross section.
